title: The Magic of Functions 📦
description: Learn to package your code into reusable blocks.

slides.title: What is a Function? 🏭
slides.content:
# The Code Factory

Imagine you own a toy factory. You don't build a new machine every time you need a single toy. You build a machine ONCE, and then turn it on whenever you need it!

A **Function** is exactly like that machine. 

It is a self-contained block of code designed to perform a specific task. You write the code once, give it a name, and then you can "call" it to run as many times as you want!

slides.title: Defining a Function 🏗️
Calling the Function 📞
Try It Out 💻
slides.content: Notice how the code inside `greetUser` does not run until we specifically call it ON line 5!

quizData.question: What happens when you write a function block (define it), but forget to call it?

options.text: The code inside the function never runs
It executes at the very end of the file automatically

quizData.explanation: Defining a function just packages the code up so it can be used later. If you never 'call' it using parentheses, it will sit there completely dormant!

slides.title: Why Use Functions? 🤔
slides.content:
## Real World Use Cases

Why go through the hassle?

1. **Reusability (DRY!)**: If you have 20 lines of code that calculate tax, you don't want to copy-paste it 50 times. Write it in a function once!
2. **Readability**: Instead of looking at 500 lines of messy math, you can just read `calculateTaxes();` and know exactly what that line does.
3. **Easy Tweaks**: If the tax rate changes, you only update the code inside the single function, instead of changing it 50 times across your file!

quizData.question: Which of these is the correct way to 'call' a function named 'startEngine'?
quizData.explanation: To invoke or call a function, simply state the function name followed by () parentheses. The parentheses act as the execute trigger.
